source_title: Dante, Virgil, and the World That Chooses Hell
summary: This episode makes guidance, authority, and escape into a durable lens family by showing that a necessary guide can rescue the soul while carrying the world that later must be escaped.

existing_lens_links.episode_phrase: Virgil is both the trusted guide and the author of the world Dante must overcome.
existing_lens_links.reason: The episode directly supports the reusable guide-as-trap mechanism already exposed as a lens point.

concept_extensions.addition: Hell is not only an afterlife destination; it is a world of piety, obedience, empire, reciprocity, and love treated as disease.
Poetry enters memory like a virus, creating dissonance and remaking perception instead of merely decorating thought.

new_lens_seeds.title: The Guide Who Becomes A Trap
new_lens_seeds.mechanism: Mediation becomes dangerous when the guide's world is smuggled into the rescue.
new_lens_seeds.why_reusable: The mechanism can apply beyond Dante to teachers, institutions, ideologies, experts, traditions, and agents that orient a person while installing the terms of captivity.

atlas_mutations.reason: The episode shows that leaving a false world is not only an attention or belief problem; it is also a mediation problem, because rescue can carry the grammar of the trap.

chronology_notes.note: As of 2026-04-08, the processed corpus has a late Great Books formulation of the guide-as-trap mechanism. Older processed sources may later become earlier foundations or change the genealogy.

next_actions: Keep episode marks linked to lens-point:guide-trap-necessary-guide where the phrase directly invokes the mechanism.
When older lectures are processed, check whether they contain earlier formulations of trusted mediation, false guides, or authority as trap.
Use this impact file as the first template for backfilling corpus impact on existing published episodes.
